hero: Imagine reading what someone says — with the sound switched off. When you speak, the muscles of your face and throat move, and a moving muscle gives off a tiny pulse of electricity, right at the skin. Sensors resting on the skin pick up those pulses, and from them we recover the words you meant — even if you only mouth them, making no sound. By the end, four words in five come back right — this is how.
why: The science here lives in three dense research papers — walls of math most people never read. They come from one lab at the University of California, Davis — Harshavardhana Gowda, Lee Miller, and colleagues — who built the whole approach: reading speech from the muscles of the face. They created that foundation; this project builds one step higher, taking their result and pushing it much further — which is what the rest of these pages walk through. So this is that same science, made to watch instead of decode — about fifty short scenes, one idea each.
landscape: How do you read speech straight from the body — through one of three doors. The first is a brain implant — electrodes placed inside the brain: the strongest, cleanest signal, and it works even for someone who cannot move a muscle — but it takes open-skull surgery. The second reads the brain from outside, through a cap on the scalp — no surgery, but the skull blurs everything, so reading the speech you produce barely works. The third is surface E M G — small sensors resting on the skin of the face and neck, reading the muscles directly: no surgery, a strong, sharp, safe signal — implant-grade access to the speech muscles without ever opening the skull. The one catch: you must be able to move your face, so it won't replace an implant for the fully paralyzed; but for everyone who can still move, it is the safe, promising middle path.
roadmap: Here's the whole journey, before we begin. We start at the body and its signal, tidied into fingerprints — one per snapshot. A small network learns to read them as sounds, lined up without a clock, and sounds become words on a map of English. Then two readers and a language model pick the best sentence — ending at the score — eighteen and a half words wrong in a hundred: about fifty short scenes, each one idea.
muscles: Before a single word leaves your mouth, your brain has to move it there. To speak, your brain fires electrical commands down nerves to dozens of muscles across your face, jaw and throat. The ring around your lips — the orbicularis oris — pulls them into an O. Your cheeks lift with the zygomaticus, and the masseter, the slab over your jaw, clamps it shut. Your tongue rides the genioglossus, and low in the throat strap muscles like the sternohyoid steady your larynx. So a single sound isn't one muscle — it's dozens, firing in a precise sequence. And here's the key: a muscle contracts because its fibres fire electrically — so every contraction is itself a tiny burst of electricity.
signal: That burst is real: to contract, each muscle fibre fires a tiny electrical pulse, and together they leak a faint voltage out onto the skin. It's about a thousandth of a volt — far too faint to feel, but a sensor resting on the skin can read it. Recording muscle electricity from the skin has a name: EMG — electromyography. Electro for electricity, myo for muscle, graphy for recording. One sensor hears every nearby muscle blurred together, so the rig uses thirty-one of them across the face and neck. Each sensor is one channel — one stream of voltage readings, taken five thousand times a second. While it records, a microphone captures the real voice alongside — but only while building the system; at real use, the microphone is gone. Here's what comes back: thirty-one wiggly lines, thousands of points a second, overlapping, mostly noise. And the data is one corpus from a single speaker — nine thousand six hundred and sixty sentences; about eighty-five hundred to learn from and a few hundred to test, decoded against a dictionary of thirty-four thousand words. Hidden inside that noise is every word. The rest of this story is one question: how do we pull them back out?
features-window: We've got thirty-one wiggly traces pouring in nonstop — but what comes next has to compare them, and you can't compare an endless stream. So first, make every trace speak the same loudness, so a strong sensor can't shout over a quiet one. Now watch a little window, just twenty-five milliseconds wide, slide along and take a snapshot at each step. Look closely: each step moves only twenty milliseconds, so every snapshot shares five milliseconds with the one before — nothing falls through the cracks. Repeat that all the way across, and the flowing stream settles into a steady, even rhythm. Fifty snapshots every second — the endless stream is now frames, like a film.
two-sensors-dancing: Forget all thirty-one sensors for a moment — watch just two. Picture them as dancers, and ask one question: when one steps up, does the other step up with it? Here they rise and fall as a pair — they move together. Now flip one: as the first rises, the second drops — they move against. And sometimes one wanders while the other ignores it — no relationship at all. Notice the trick: turn the second dancer down, and whether they move together or against still reads the same — only its strength shrinks. So it isn't really about how loud either one is — it's whether they move together or against, and that's the heart of what we call covariance.
features-cov: You just saw one pair of sensors, and one number for how together they move. But one pair can't tell two sounds apart. So you run that same test for every pair of sensors, filling a thirty-one by thirty-one grid. Each bright cell means those two sensors moved as a team. The whole grid is this snapshot's signature: which muscles pulled together, right now. Watch: two different gestures light up two completely different patterns.
every-pair-grid: You just got one number for two sensors. But there are thirty-one. So ask the very same question of every pair: each sensor against all thirty-one, itself included. Drop each answer into its own slot — row picks one sensor, column picks the other. Sweep through, and the whole table fills in. Down the diagonal a sensor meets itself, so those just measure its own size. It is the off-diagonal cells that tell you who moved with whom. One snapshot of the face becomes one whole grid.
features-embed: But each grid was built from one tiny snapshot of time, so its numbers come out jittery and unreliable. So we nudge every grid a little toward one calm, plain pattern — ninety percent the measured grid, ten percent the plain one. Now it's steadier — but it's still nine hundred sixty-one numbers, which is a lot to carry. So we funnel each grid down to just three hundred eighty-four numbers: one compact fingerprint per snapshot. Do that for every snapshot, and the whole sentence becomes a filmstrip of fingerprints. This is what the reader actually reads.
what-is-a-net: A fingerprint goes in; a guessed letter should come out. But what's the thing in the middle that does the reading? Picture a function — a deep stack of layers, packed with millions of tiny dials. Numbers flow in the left, get reshaped at every dial, and a guess falls out the right. Here's the strange part: nobody writes those dials by hand. At the start they're set at random, so the very first guess is pure nonsense. Everything the network will ever know is just how those dials end up turned. So the only question left is how they get turned.
how-training-works: So the box guesses garbage — but here's the thing: for this example, you already know the right answer. Compare the two, and measure exactly how wrong the guess is — call that the error. Now the trick: picture that error as the height of a hill, and all the dials together as where you're standing on it. From up the slope you can always feel which way is downhill — the way that makes the error smaller. Take one small step that way, and the guess gets a little less wrong. Show another example and step again, millions of times, and you roll to the valley floor. That settling at the bottom is the whole of training: shrink the error.
targets-phon: Last step we said: show it an example with a known answer. But the answer to what — what should the model write down? The obvious guess is letters. Watch what letters do: though, tough, through — same ending, three different sounds. English spelling lies, so letters are a shaky thing to predict. So instead of spelling cat, we break it into the sounds you actually make — a K, the a-as-in-cat, a T. There's a small fixed set — about forty in the version we use, the building blocks of every English word. And here is the payoff: chain those forty sounds and you can build a word the model has never once seen.
targets-units: Last time we gave it about forty sounds to aim for. But forty buckets is coarse — a lot of different noises get rounded into the same one. So what if we gave it a second, finer set of sounds: a hundred of them. Now one of those forty can split apart into the several finer shapes hiding inside it. And we add one more symbol, a blank, that just means no new sound landed in this snapshot. Where did these hundred come from? A separate model found them on its own — that's the next story. For now, just feel why two answer keys beat one: the training goal leans mostly on the fine sounds, a little on the forty, and ties the two together so they never disagree.
where-units-from: Those hundred finer sounds — who decided on them? Nobody did. Here's the trick. You take a fresh model and play it endless plain audio — no labels, just sound. Then you hide a little snapshot, and its only job is to guess what was behind the gap, using what surrounds it. Wrong at first, but the more it practices, the closer its guesses get. And to get good at guessing, it has to quietly sort sounds into bins of its own — this kind of hiss, that kind of hum. Nobody named those bins. It found them. Those recurring shapes became the hundred units.
two-answer-keys: So the same stream of snapshots now gets graded against two answer keys at once. The top key is the forty sounds: coarse, clean, the names humans gave them. The bottom key is the hundred units: finer, stranger, texture the machine found on its own. Now watch a lazy guess that blurs three different sounds into one — the top key shrugs and passes it, but the fine units catch the smear. And a slip the units miss is exactly the kind the forty flag. So to pass, a guess has to be right on both keys — and being right on two keys at once is far harder to fake than one. It's like revising from two sets of notes: only what they agree on is real. And notice — neither key ever says when each sound happens.
alignment-problem: We have the sounds of every sentence, in order. But here is what we don't have. The recording is fifty snapshots a second, and a short word like cat is just three sounds. So which snapshot holds the K? Which one holds the T? Nothing in the data says. It's like a movie's subtitles with every timestamp erased: you know the words, you know their order, you just can't tell which snapshot each one lands on. So we can't hand the model the timing. It has to discover it.
ctc: We know the word, but not which snapshot is which sound. So here's the trick: don't pin sounds to snapshots at all. For every single snapshot, the model just guesses — a sound, or a blank that means "nothing here yet." Read those guesses left to right: blank, K, K, blank, AE, AE, AE, T, blank. Now collapse them. Merge any sound that repeats, then throw the blanks away. And there it is — the word "cat" falls out. The quiet magic: a faster or slower saying of the same word collapses to that exact same answer. So the model never needs the timing. It only has to get the order right.
ctc-many-timings: So how do you train this when nobody labeled which snapshot is which sound? Here is the trick. The same word, "cat", can be spelled by many different timings — stretch the sounds, squeeze them, sprinkle in blanks. Each of those timings has its own odds of being what the model heard. Instead of betting on one true timing, you simply add all those odds into a single total: the chance of "cat". Training does one thing — it pushes that total up, and every valid timing rises together. Get the order wrong, and you get no push at all. So no single timing is ever called the truth — they all win together, and the right order falls out on its own.
encoder-bidir: Here's one snapshot, and its sound is a toss-up — could be a D, could be a T. On its own, the reader can't tell. But the snapshots just before it carry a clue, so let it peek backward — that's all a live caption can do, since the future hasn't been spoken yet. The guess is sharper, but still hedged. Now — this is a finished recording, so the snapshots after it already exist too. Let it look both ways, and the toss-up snaps to a clear T. Seeing before and after isn't a tweak — it's what actually pulls the error rate down.
what-is-attention: Looking both ways was the gentle version; here is the real tool. Pick one blurry snapshot — which sound is it? Let it reach out and touch every other snapshot in the sentence, near and far. Each one answers with a weight: how relevant are you to me? Most say barely; a few say a lot. The blurry snapshot rebuilds itself from those few — and snaps into a clear sound. Notice the loudest answer came from far away: distance simply does not matter here. Now let every snapshot do this at once, each pulling on its own handful. Reaching everywhere and weighing by relevance — that move is called attention.
what-is-convolution: Attention just did something amazing: to name one blurry snapshot, it reached clear across the whole sentence. But reaching that far has a cost. When you average over everything, a tiny detail in one snapshot — a quick onset, a sharp little edge — gets smeared flat. So give the reader a second lens: a small magnifier that slides along, looking only at a snapshot and its closest neighbours. At each step it reads just that little patch, and out pops the sharp local texture the wide view missed. That sliding little-window lens has a name: convolution. Reach far for context, magnify for detail — and a reader that runs both lenses at once is exactly the Conformer.
memorize-trap: We have a lot of data, but it all comes from one person — so it runs out. Hand it to a giant reader and watch what it does: it threads a curve through every single example, perfectly. Looks like a genius. Now show it one example it never saw — and it's wildly off. It didn't learn the subject; it memorized the answer key, then crumbled on a new question. Shrink the reader down to just four layers, and it has far less room to memorize — so it leans on the simple pattern underneath. And that smaller reader is the one that gets the new example right.
conformer: You just met two ways of reading: one that reaches across the whole sentence, and one that studies the fine detail right around each moment. What if the reader used both at the very same time? Watch — the far reach and the close-up window land on one snapshot together. That fused block is the reader; we call it a Conformer. And we keep it tiny — only four layers — because with just one person's data, a bigger reader would only memorize.
heads: The reader has finished reading one snapshot. Watch what comes out: it gives three answers at once. The first is a vote over a hundred fine units. The second is a vote over forty sounds — and this one everything downstream relies on. Now watch the third. It never picks a winner. It's just a stream of a thousand numbers — not for reading text at all. Its only job is to imitate a real human voice. Here is why that matters.
copy-the-teacher: Remember the reader's third output — the one that refused to vote, and just glowed? It exists for a clever trick. Normally, when you teach a model, you tell it only one thing: was your final answer right or wrong. But picture a master painter and a student copying one stroke. If you only check where the line ends, the student can finish in the right spot while the whole curve in between is wrong. So instead, lay tracing paper over the master's stroke, and match its entire shape, point by point. Now the student doesn't just hit the endpoint — its whole line slides onto the master's, and the distance between them falls sharply. That's distillation: copy the teacher's rich internal picture, not just its final verdict.
voice-as-teacher: We said a teacher exists. But who is it? Here is the trick: while the muscle signal was being recorded, a microphone ran right beside the sensors, catching the real voice. A big audio model — one that already listened to an ocean of speech — turns that voice into a rich, structured picture, deep in its ninth layer. The reader's third output is simply trained to copy that picture. And here is the catch worth holding onto: this mostly sharpens the raw sounds, not the words yet. Then at real use, the microphone is gone — only the lesson stays.
distillation: So you have the teacher's picture and the student's blurry guess, sitting side by side. The simplest pull is obvious: make the student's numbers match the teacher's, frame by frame. Watch the gap between them collapse toward zero. But "match the numbers" has a quiet trap — the safest way to be close to everyone is to land on a dull average. So we add a second pull: each moment must claim its own match and shove every other moment away. Together, in equal measure: copy the numbers, and keep each moment distinct.
frozen-recognizer: Copying the voice has a loophole. Take two sounds that are nearly alike — say an "s" and a "z." The easy way to score well on both is to land halfway between them, on a single mushy point. The score looks great, but that point is neither sound — it can't be read back as either one. So we bolt on a sound-reader and freeze it: a fixed checker the model is not allowed to soften. Now the blur fails, and the only way to pass is to pull the two sounds back apart — close to the voice, and still readable as the right sound.
blurry-average-cheat: The student copies one long string of numbers, and its only job is to land close to the teacher's. Now suppose two different sounds sit a little apart in that space. What's the laziest way to stay close to both at once? Plant one point dead in the middle — equidistant, so the copy-score looks great. But that middle point is neither sound. It's a smooth blur that scores well and means nothing. The two sounds you needed to tell apart have quietly melted into one.
frozen-lock-how: So the cheat was that "be close" let the blur score well — how do you catch it? You add a second judge that doesn't grade closeness at all; it only asks one question: can two real, distinct sounds be read out of these features? And here's the trick — you bolt this sound-reader on and freeze it solid, dials locked, so the student can't soften it or bend its answer. Feed it the mushy blur, and it stalls: it can't tell which sound, so it fails outright. The only way to pass is to pull the blur back into two crisp sounds the reader can actually name. So the features end up close to the voice and genuinely decodable — caught by a judge that cannot be bought.
twin-warmstart: We have one good reader. So why on earth would we build a second one? Watch the new reader cheat: instead of starting blank, it copies the front-end and the heads straight from the model we already trained. Only its middle starts empty, and that part trains up fast. Now the twist: we grow a second reader from the same blueprint, but train it differently — stronger jitter, heavier dropout, and a different audio teacher. Both end up just as accurate, stuck at the same twenty-point-nine percent wall. But look where each one slips: they break on different words. Two readers, same ceiling, different cracks — and that gap is about to become useful.
second-opinion: You just built two readers that hit the same wall — so why keep both? Here is the payoff. Picture the true words as a line, and each reader as a wobbly guess around it. Reader A guesses too high right where reader B guesses too low. Average the two, point by point, and those opposite mistakes cancel — the average hugs the truth. The word error rate falls from twenty-six down to about twenty. But this only works because they err in different places: two readers that make the same mistakes average to the same mistake, and gain nothing. That is the whole point of the tougher twin — not to be better, but to be different. Different mistakes are the asset.
wfst: The reader hands us a stream of sounds — but sounds still are not words. So we lay out a giant map of words where every road spells a real word, and every road charges a toll for how unlikely it is. We send the sounds in and light up the single cheapest path from start to end — and out fall the words. Here is the trick: that one enormous map is really three smaller maps stitched together — how to collapse the raw sounds, how thirty-four thousand words are spelled, and how English words tend to follow each other. Because the map carries that whole dictionary, it knows five times more words than we ever trained on. So it can spell out words it has never once seen.
sounds-to-words-search: You've got a stream of sounds, but a hundred different sentences could fit them. So which one? That map of words is already laid out: each way the sounds could spell a word is a fork in the road. Give every step a toll — cheap when the sounds and the grammar agree, steep when they fight. Now a whole sentence is just one route across the map, and what it costs is the sum of its tolls. The gibberish roads pile up huge tolls and you'd never take them. So the best reading is simply the cheapest way across — and finding the cheapest route is something computers are already great at.
knobs: The same string of sounds can be "ice cream" or "I scream." The cheapest-path search you just saw has to pick one, and two dials tip the scale. The first dial sets how much to trust the muscle reading versus plain English; turn it down near zero point two five and most wrong words snap right. But the reader has a lazy habit: frame after frame, it would rather guess blank than commit to a sound. The second dial charges a toll on blank, near two point zero, forcing it to commit. Set either dial wrong, and almost every word comes out wrong.
what-is-lm: Say "the peanut," and stop. Your mind already leans toward "butter," while "bicycle" feels absurd. Something has read so much text it does the same — given the words so far, it ranks which word likely comes next: butter bright, bicycle dim. Now the search reuses that ranking as its toll — likely words become cheap roads, wild ones expensive, so the cheapest route bends toward sensible English. And here's the strange part: it has never heard a sound. It only reads — a next-word guesser, learned from mountains of text. The map of words uses a small one to score; a far bigger one chooses later.
ensemble-nbest: You have two readers of the muscle signal, and right now each hands you one sentence that's wrong about a quarter of the time. You already saw the first idea: average them, frame by frame — where one reader wobbles, the other holds steady, and the noise cancels. That nudges you from twenty-six percent wrong down to about twenty. But notice you're still demanding a single answer. So stop: instead of the top sentence, keep a whole shortlist — dozens of candidate sentences, and pool the lists you get at every dial setting. Now watch where the truly correct sentence lands: almost always somewhere in that pool, even when it isn't your number-one pick. If you could always reach in and grab the best candidate, you'd be wrong just nine point three percent of the time — yet today you pick one that's wrong about twenty. That gap is the prize: the right answer is already here; you just need something smarter to find it.
what-is-wer: We keep saying twenty percent wrong, eighteen percent wrong — but wrong measured how? Take the sentence the model guessed, and line it up under the true one, like grading a dictation. Now count the fixes. One word is wrong — swap it. One word is extra — drop it. One word is missing — add it. Three fixes, over eight words truly spoken — call it three eighths wrong. Fix even one, and the score falls — so lower is always the goal. That score has a name: word error rate.
rerank: So the right sentence is already in the pool — we just need something to reach in and pick it out. That something is the chooser: a language model with seven billion learned patterns, squeezed small enough to run on one modest machine. We hand it the top ten guesses and the detected sounds at the same time. Two guesses look almost identical — "quick" or "quiet" — and the sounds carry a hard k. So the chooser leans on that k and picks "the quick brown fox." And we checked one limit honestly: the chooser only ever picks from those ten guesses — it can't recover the right sentence if the pool missed it.
binding-constraint: One honest catch, before we celebrate the climb. The chooser is brilliant — but it can only pick from the short list of guesses it is handed. On the hardest sentences, the right word was never in the sounds the reader heard. So no chooser, however clever, can reach for a word that simply is not there. That is why the word-error levels off near eighteen and a half and refuses to go lower. The real ceiling is the sound-error, holding firm around twenty point nine. To break below it, you would need a sharper reader — not a smarter chooser.
journey: Now watch one thought travel the whole machine, left to right. You speak. Thirty-one sensors feel the faint electricity on your face and neck. Each snapshot of signal becomes a little fingerprint. The reader turns those fingerprints into sounds. The map of words turns sounds into a pool of likely sentences. And the chooser picks the words you meant. Everything you have just learned, in order, lighting up as it goes.
arc: Here is the whole climb, in one chart: out of every hundred words, fifty-one came back wrong. Move one fixed how sounds get turned into words — fifty-one drops to about forty. Move two let the reader look both ways through the signal and copy the teacher voice — forty falls all the way to twenty-six. Move three pooled the readers and let the chooser pick — twenty-six down to eighteen and a half. Now watch the faint line underneath — that is the sound error, the part that depends only on what the reader heard. On move two it plunges: the reader truly heard better. On move three it barely budges — that drop was smarter word-picking, not better hearing.
approach: So what, exactly, is new here? I didn't start from scratch — I took a published system that got fifty-one percent of words wrong, and changed three things. One: I fixed the decode — recovering the acoustic scale and blank penalty the public release left out, on the same full-dictionary decoder. Two: I let the reader see the whole sentence at once, taught during training by the matching teacher voice. Three: I ran two of those readers, pooled their guesses, and let a language model with seven billion learned patterns pick the best line. Three changes. Fifty-one percent wrong, down to eighteen and a half.
silent: So what is all of this for? You mouth the words — no breath, no sound. And it types them. Dictate in a silent room. Say something private with people all around you. Get a message through where a microphone would only hear noise. It was trained on one healthy speaker, so think of it as a silent keyboard, not a medical device. Making it work for many people is the road still ahead.
foundation: Before we say goodbye, look at the ground all of this stood on. The recording corpus, the thirty-one channel sensor array, the geometric features, the E M G to text decoder — every one of those came out of U C Davis. My whole pipeline is just one small block resting on that work. So the credit goes to Harshavardhana T Gowda, with Zachary D McNaughton, Daniel C Comstock, and Lee M Miller. I didn't start from zero. I only built upward.
credits: Look back at every piece I just used. The Conformer, WavLM, the HuBERT units, the C T C training — each one came from someone else's published work. So did the decoder, the language model, Qwen, and QLoRA. I invented none of them. What I did was choose them, and fit them together. This is composition, not invention. And the look of these animations is an homage to 3Blue1Brown, who showed that ideas can be watched, not just read.
thanks: That's the whole journey — from faint electricity on the skin to words on a screen. Thank you for making it all the way to the end. This was made by Varshith Madishetty. And built with the help of Claude.
end: From silent muscle to readable text — that was the whole idea. The end.
